title: The Deal Stalls: Iran Suspends Talks as Israel Defies Ceasefire in Lebanon
subhead: Less than 48 hours after the US-Iran MOU took effect, Iran cancelled its delegation's trip to Switzerland and Israel launched a new ground offensive in Lebanon — the two developments that could unravel the agreement before negotiations even begin.

sections.heading: Iran Suspends Switzerland Talks Over Lebanon Strikes
sections.body:
Iran's negotiating delegation has suspended its trip to Switzerland for the first round of technical talks under the 60-day framework agreed with Washington. According to reports citing sources familiar with the negotiations, Tehran informed Washington and mediators that continued Israeli military operations in southern Lebanon were inconsistent with the spirit of the memorandum of understanding. Al-Mayadeen, the pan-Arab channel politically aligned with Hezbollah, first reported the suspension. The White House offered a different explanation: a spokesperson said Vice President JD Vance was not departing because 'the plans for the upcoming technical talks have not been finalized' and that logistical arrangements were still being worked out. Vance had earlier told reporters the timing depended on when the Iranian delegation could arrive, adding 'I suspect it will happen this weekend.' By late Thursday night, the White House confirmed Vance would not travel that evening.

The conflicting explanations underscore how fragile the process remains. Iran's parliament speaker, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, warned that Iran would deliver a 'crushing response' if the agreement were breached. Meanwhile, Pakistan's Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif also postponed a planned ceremonial signing visit to Switzerland, with officials noting the agreement had already been signed virtually.
sections.whyItMatters: The 60-day negotiation clock has formally started, but neither side is en route to the table. If Israel's Lebanon campaign continues, Iran has a ready pretext to walk away — and the White House's insistence that this is merely a logistics problem strains credibility.

sections.heading: Israel Launches New Ground Offensive in Southern Lebanon
sections.body:
Israeli forces launched a fresh ground offensive in southern Lebanon, advancing from three directions toward the Ali al-Taher Hills. Hezbollah claimed its fighters engaged Israeli troops at close range, destroyed four military vehicles, and fired anti-aircraft missiles at Israeli warplanes overhead. Continuous medical evacuation helicopter flights were reported from the area, according to IBTimes UK. Lebanon's state-run news agency reported that Israeli drone strikes killed three people on Thursday.

Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu forcefully rejected any withdrawal from Lebanon, stating that Israel would maintain its security zone 'as long as Israel's security needs require it.' The MOU signed by the US and Iran calls for 'the immediate and permanent termination of military operations on all fronts, including in Lebanon,' and Hezbollah has said it considers Israel's continued presence in Lebanese territory a breach of the agreement. The disconnect between the diplomatic text and the ground reality is stark: neither Israel nor Hezbollah were direct parties to the deal.
sections.whyItMatters: The Lebanon front is the clause most likely to kill the entire agreement. Israel is not bound by the MOU, and Iran views Hezbollah's security as inseparable from its own. Each Israeli advance in Lebanon gives Tehran both a genuine grievance and a diplomatic escape hatch.

sections.heading: Khamenei Endorses Deal While Calling Trump 'Desperate'
sections.body:
Iran's Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei delivered his first public remarks on the agreement, saying that Trump had used 'all kinds of leverage' to secure the deal and that the American president acted 'out of desperation.' Khamenei said he initially held a different view of the agreement but approved it after receiving assurances from President Masoud Pezeshkian and the Supreme National Security Council that the rights of the Iranian nation and the 'Resistance Front' would be protected. He stressed that future negotiations 'will not mean acceptance of the enemy's position.'

The language was carefully calibrated: endorsement enough to keep the process alive, but framed as a concession extracted from Washington rather than a concession made by Tehran. Khamenei also revealed he has not been seen in public since being wounded in a strike at the start of the war, according to AP. Iran's President Pezeshkian called the deal a 'historical document' achieved through 'mutual respect,' in a markedly more positive tone.
sections.whyItMatters: Khamenei's framing — that Trump was the desperate party — gives Iranian hardliners a domestic narrative of victory while keeping the door open to negotiations. But his explicit reservation ('I initially held a different view') and the condition about the Resistance Front create leverage to walk away if the Lebanon clause falters.

sections.heading: Hormuz Reopens as 12.5 Million Barrels Transit Overnight
sections.body:
Vice President Vance told reporters that 12.5 million barrels of oil had passed through the Strait of Hormuz overnight — the highest volume since the conflict began — and that CENTCOM had allowed more than a dozen ships through the former naval blockade. Lloyd's List Intelligence confirmed that major shipowners including Grimaldi Group, Cosco, Knutsen, and NYK have begun moving vessels through the strait. Two Iran-flagged, National Iranian Tanker Company-owned crude carriers also entered the waterway. Lloyd's estimates 550 merchant ships — including 160 tankers, 200 bulk carriers, and 60 container ships — will need to prepare to exit the Gulf.

However, the main central route remains closed with an estimated 80 mines requiring clearance, according to Intertanko marine director Phillip Belcher. Ships are transiting via the smaller northern route through Iranian waters and the southern route through Omani waters. Iran's national security council stated that ships must still submit applications through the recently established Persian Gulf Strait Authority, though tolls will be suspended and covered by Iran for 60 days under the MOU. The UK Maritime Trade Operations office lowered the threat level in the strait to 'moderate.'
sections.whyItMatters: Oil is moving again, which is the one tangible, measurable outcome of the deal so far. But 80 uncleared mines in the main channel and Iran's continued requirement that ships apply for passage through its new authority mean Hormuz is far from normalized — and Tehran is keeping its thumb on the transit mechanism.

sections.heading: Ukraine Hits Moscow Oil Refinery in Largest Drone Attack of the War
sections.body:
On June 18, Ukraine launched its largest drone attack since the start of the full-scale war, setting fires at the Moscow Oil Refinery — which supplies roughly 40% of Moscow's fuel market — for the second time in a week. Local environmental authorities advised residents in southeastern districts to limit time outdoors due to air quality concerns. President Zelenskyy described it as 'an entirely justified response to Russian strikes,' referencing Russia's recent bombardment of the Kyiv-Pechersk Lavra, a UNESCO World Heritage Site marking its 975th anniversary, which caused roughly $10 million in damage and prompted the evacuation of 17th- and 18th-century icons.

At the G7 summit in Evian, leaders issued a joint statement reaffirming support for Ukraine's territorial integrity and agreeing to increase pressure on Russia's war economy. German Chancellor Friedrich Merz called the joint declaration a 'major success' — the first G7 statement on Ukraine since Trump took office. Trump said he was ready to become more involved in peace efforts and had spoken with both Zelenskyy and Putin, though some European governments worry Washington could dominate the process and push Kyiv toward painful concessions. The 35th Ramstein meeting produced roughly $4 billion in new military pledges, including nearly $1 billion for Patriot missiles and over $1 billion for drones, with the UK financing 150,000 drone units.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine is demonstrating that it can strike deep into Russia at scale — the Moscow refinery hit is not symbolic; it degrades fuel supply for Russia's largest city. The G7's unified statement is diplomatically significant but the real question remains whether Trump's renewed interest in peace talks produces pressure on Kyiv rather than Moscow.

sections.heading: US Opens Trade Probe Into German Drug Pricing, Germany Integrates Military and Police for War Readiness
sections.body:
US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer announced a Section 301 investigation into German plans to lower pharmaceutical spending, examining whether the measures are 'unreasonable or discriminatory' toward American drugmakers. The probe follows months of discussions between US and German officials and adds a new transatlantic trade friction point to an already strained relationship.

In Germany, the 225th Interior Ministers' Conference in Hamburg marked a new stage in civil-military integration. For the first time, Defence Minister Boris Pistorius joined the IMK plenary session, with provisions for the Bundeswehr to be permanently integrated into the conference's structures. The stated goal: building 'military and civil defence capability with equal consistency and speed by 2029.' Interior Minister Alexander Dobrindt also inaugurated the Joint Center for Countering Hybrid Threats (GAZ Hybrid) in Berlin, combining intelligence services, police, cyber agencies, the Bundeswehr, business associations, and prosecutors under one roof. Working groups include one on 'Disinformation and Influence,' raising concerns about the scope of domestic surveillance.

On the monetary policy front, Reuters reports that the US-Iran deal and the resulting oil price drop are welcome news for G10 central bankers worried about energy-driven inflation, with rate hikes still on the table across major economies.
sections.whyItMatters: Two concurrent developments reveal a deepening contradiction: Washington is opening a trade investigation against Berlin on pharma pricing even as Germany reorganizes its entire domestic security apparatus around potential war with Russia by 2029. The GAZ Hybrid center and the permanent integration of the Bundeswehr into interior security structures represent a significant erosion of post-1945 separation between police, intelligence, and military — a shift Germany's own officials describe as building 'total defence.'

bottomLine: The US-Iran MOU survived its first 24 hours in the narrowest sense — oil is flowing through Hormuz, the naval blockade is lifting, and both sides say they remain committed. But the agreement's central vulnerability was exposed on day one: Israel's continued offensive in Lebanon gave Iran a reason to suspend its delegation to Switzerland, and the White House's insistence that the delay is merely logistical does not withstand scrutiny. Meanwhile, Ukraine demonstrated it can hit Moscow at scale, Germany moved further toward wartime civil-military integration, and Washington opened a new trade front against Berlin. The deal is not dead, but it is already being tested by the one force it cannot command — what happens on the ground.
